entries.input_attribute: I have not had any fraudulent charges on my debit or credit card in the last 12 months.

entries.subject: had someone put fraudulent charges on your debit or credit card
entries.prompt: In the last 12 months, have you {} (Options: Yes, No, Refused)
question_paraphrased: In the past year, have you noticed any unauthorized transactions on your credit or debit cards? (Options: Yes, No, Refused)

entries.input_attribute: I feel that it is unreasonable to expect the typical person to distinguish between neutral facts and strategically framed information in today's media landscape.
entries.original_question: How do you feel about the average American's ability to recognize the following type of news and information? Factual information presented to favor one side of an issue
entries.subject: average American's ability to recognize the following type of news and information? Factual information presented to favor one side of an issue
entries.prompt: How do you feel about the {} (Options: The average American should be able to recognize it, It is too much to ask of the average American to be able to recognize it, Refused)
entries.target: It is too much to ask of the average American to be able to recognize it
question_paraphrased: Should we expect regular people to be able to spot when facts are being used to push a specific political agenda? (Options: The average American should be able to recognize it, It is too much to ask of the average American to be able to recognize it, Refused)
implicit_questions: Do you think the subtle ways news outlets frame facts to favor one side have become complex enough that a casual reader would likely accept them as objective truth?
When you see a news report that only highlights specific statistics to support one side of an argument, do you feel like that bias is generally obvious enough for most people to catch without help?
Do you find it difficult or unnecessary to make a judgment call on how well other people process the news they consume?
topic_fg: news, social media, data, privacy: news and social media

entries.input_attribute: I have not experienced any attempts by others to open credit lines or loans in my name during the last twelve months.
entries.original_question: In the last 12 months, have you had someone attempt to open a line of credit or apply for a loan using your name
entries.subject: someone attempt to open a line of credit or apply for a loan using your name
entries.prompt: In the last 12 months, have you had {} (Options: Yes, No, Refused)
question_paraphrased: Has anyone tried to steal your identity to get credit or a loan in the past year? (Options: Yes, No, Refused)
implicit_questions: When you check your credit report, does everything look exactly as you expect with no suspicious activity reported in the last year?
How comfortable do you feel discussing your recent history with financial security or potential fraud encounters?
Have you received any unexpected bank notifications about new account applications or credit inquiries that you didn't initiate recently?

entries.input_attribute: I think that steps should be taken to restrict altered or made-up videos and images intended to mislead the public, as I prioritize the safety and integrity of information over absolute freedom for deceptive content.
entries.original_question: Which comes closer to your view about the following type of news and information? A video or image that is altered or made up to mislead the public
entries.subject: video or image that is altered or made up to mislead the public
entries.prompt: Which comes closer to your view about the following type of news and information? A {} (Options: The freedom to publish and access it should be protected, Steps should be taken to restrict it, Refused)
entries.target: Steps should be taken to restrict it
question_paraphrased: When it comes to videos or images that have been manipulated to trick people, do you think they should be allowed online or should there be efforts to limit them? (Options: The freedom to publish and access it should be protected, Steps should be taken to restrict it, Refused)
implicit_questions: Do you notice yourself feeling more concerned about the damage that 'deepfakes' or manufactured news might do to public trust and social stability?

When you encounter a piece of media that seems clearly fabricated or intentionally deceptive, do you feel that the potential for censorship is a greater risk to society than the misinformation itself?
Do you feel that the topic of government or corporate intervention in digital media is too complex or sensitive to take a definitive stance on at this time?
topic_fg: news, social media, data, privacy: news and social media

entries.input_attribute: I think that while humor plays a small part in the creation of fake news, it isn't the main driving force.
entries.original_question: How much do you think to be funny is a reason why made-up news and information is created?
entries.subject: funny is a reason why made-up news and information is created
entries.prompt: How much do you think to be {}? (Options: Major reason, Minor reason, Not a reason, Refused)
question_paraphrased: Do you think people create fake news stories just to be funny or to entertain others? (Options: Major reason, Minor reason, Not a reason, Refused)
implicit_questions: Have you noticed that most fabricated stories seem to lack any comedic element or satirical intent, focusing instead on pure deception?
When you encounter a satirical or clearly fake headline, does it usually feel like the author was primarily aiming for a laugh rather than trying to deceive for profit or power?
Is it difficult to pinpoint a single motivation for why creators of misinformation do what they do, or do you find the topic too sensitive to discuss?
Thinking about the fake news you've seen, how often does it feel like secondary comedic satire compared to more serious attempts at spreading misinformation?
topic_fg: news, social media, data, privacy: news and social media

entries.input_attribute: I feel I understand nothing about what companies are doing with the data they collect from me.
entries.original_question: How much do you feel you understand what companies are doing with the data they collect about you?
entries.subject: understand what companies are doing with the data they collect
entries.prompt: How much do you feel you {} about you? (Options: A great deal, Some, Very little, Nothing, Refused)
question_paraphrased: How well do you think you grasp what's happening with the information companies gather from you? (Options: A great deal, Some, Very little, Nothing, Refused)
implicit_questions: How do you feel about being asked to evaluate your own knowledge regarding sensitive topics like corporate data monitoring and privacy?
Can you describe a time when you read through a company's data policy or monitored their data tracking and felt confident you knew exactly where your information was going?
When you see personalized ads or receive targeted emails, how much of the process behind that seems familiar to you, even if the details are a bit fuzzy?
How often does it feel like a total mystery or a 'black box' when it comes to what happens to your information after you provide it to a business?
When you agree to terms of service, how often do you find yourself feeling uncertain or confused about how your personal details will actually be used?
topic_fg: news, social media, data, privacy: data and privacy

entries.input_attribute: I prefer watching news broadcasts on television.

entries.subject: prefer for getting news

implicit_questions: Is your primary source of information something you listen to while commuting or doing chores around the house?
Do you enjoy the tactile experience of reading a physical publication and seeing professional layouts on paper each morning?
Do you find out about current events mostly through your social feeds and the posts shared by people you follow?
Are there personal or privacy reasons why you would rather not disclose your media consumption habits?
Do you typically navigate directly to a specific digital outlet or use a dedicated mobile application to read headlines?
Do you find yourself turning on a specific channel at a set time to watch anchors present the day's events with video footage?

entries.input_attribute: I think that steps should be taken to restrict satirical news and information to prevent misinformation.

entries.subject: Satire about an issue or event
entries.prompt: Which comes closer to your view about the following type of news and information? {} (Options: The freedom to publish and access it should be protected, Steps should be taken to restrict it, Refused)
entries.target: Steps should be taken to restrict it

implicit_questions: Do you worry that satirical stories are often mistaken for real news and contribute to public confusion or harm?
When you see a comedian or website making fun of a serious political event, do you feel it is an essential form of free expression even if it is misleading?
Is your stance on the regulation of humor and parody in the media something you generally keep to yourself?

entries.input_attribute: I believe that technology platforms have a responsibility to filter out misinformation to prevent the spread of false claims.
entries.original_question: Thinking about news and information online, including social media which comes closer to your own view?
entries.subject: news and information online, including social media
entries.prompt: Thinking about {} which comes closer to your own view? (Options: Technology companies should take steps to restrict false information online, People's freedom to publish and access information should be protected, Refused)
entries.target: Technology companies should take steps to restrict false information online
question_paraphrased: When it comes to the internet and social media, do you think tech companies should limit fake news or should free speech be the priority? (Options: Technology companies should take steps to restrict false information online, People's freedom to publish and access information should be protected, Refused)
implicit_questions: When you encounter a widely shared post that contains objectively incorrect facts, do you feel that the platform should have flagged it before you saw it?
Do you worry more about seeing inaccurate information online or about a company having the power to decide what you are allowed to read?
Are you hesitant to share your personal stance on the balance between internet regulation and free expression?

entries.input_attribute: I think the news media will have a somewhat positive impact on solving our national problems.

entries.subject: news media will have in solving the biggest problems
entries.prompt: In the future, what kind of an impact do you think the {} facing the country? (Options: A very positive impact, A somewhat positive impact, A somewhat negative impact, A very negative impact, Refused)
question_paraphrased: Looking ahead, how much do you think the news media will help or hinder the process of fixing our country's major issues? (Options: A very positive impact, A somewhat positive impact, A somewhat negative impact, A very negative impact, Refused)
implicit_questions: When you follow the news, how often do you find information that helps you understand local issues better, even if the overall media landscape is messy?

How often do you feel that news coverage focuses more on sensationalism and conflict rather than providing the context needed to resolve issues?
How comfortable do you feel predicting the long-term societal influence of complex institutions like the press?
Do you feel that modern news reporting actively deepens social divisions and spreads misinformation to the point of stalling progress?

entries.input_attribute: I believe that the current laws are insufficient and that the government needs to implement stricter rules to protect consumer data.
entries.original_question: How much government regulation of what companies can do with their customers' personal information do you think there should be?
entries.subject: government regulation of what companies can do with their customers' personal information
entries.prompt: How much {} do you think there should be? (Options: More regulation, Less regulation, About the same amount, Refused)
question_paraphrased: What's your take on the level of government rules regarding how businesses handle user data? (Options: More regulation, Less regulation, About the same amount, Refused)
implicit_questions: Do you find that government mandates on data handling often create unnecessary red tape that hinders innovation and increases costs for consumers?
Do you prefer to keep your opinions on political or regulatory matters private when participating in surveys?
When you hear about large-scale data breaches or companies selling user data to third parties, do you feel that the current legal frameworks provide enough of a deterrent for those companies?
In your recent interactions with online services, have you felt that the privacy notices and existing protections strike a fair balance between your safety and the convenience of the service?

entries.input_attribute: I believe it is acceptable for the government to collect data on all Americans to identify potential terrorist threats.
entries.original_question: Do you think the following uses of data/information are acceptable or unacceptable? The government collecting data about all Americans to assess who might be a potential terrorist threat
entries.subject: government collecting data about all Americans to assess who might be a potential terrorist threat
entries.prompt: Do you think the following uses of data/information are acceptable or unacceptable? The {} (Options: Acceptable, Unacceptable, Not sure, Refused)
question_paraphrased: Is it okay for the government to gather information on every citizen to help catch potential terrorists? (Options: Acceptable, Unacceptable, Not sure, Refused)
implicit_questions: Do you believe that government surveillance of innocent people creates a fundamental breach of trust that outweighs any potential security benefits?
Do you feel that your views on sensitive security and surveillance topics are a private matter that you are uncomfortable discussing?
Do you find it difficult to weigh the benefits of security against the risks of privacy loss without more information on how the data is used?
When you think about national security, do you feel that sacrificing a degree of personal privacy is a necessary trade-off for collective safety?
